And if this has inspired you to check out a different app, take a peek at the best chat alternatives that are better than Discord.
item.content:encoded:
Summary

  • Expired Discord invites and vanity URLs are being exploited to spread malware.
  • A hacker can hijack expired invites by using vanity URLs in Discord.
  • Malicious bots lead victims to fake verification pages to install malicious software.

It feels awful when you finally locate an invite link to a Discord server you've been trying to access, only to discover that it expired ages ago. However, it turns out there's one thing that's even worse: an invite link that pretends to be a permanent one. Bad actors have found a way to abuse vanity links to hijack expired invites and direct people to a malware-infested server.

As detected and analyzed by Check Point Research, this attack takes advantage of how Discord handles server invite URLs. If you've used the service before, there's a good chance you've seen one of its invites; they're URLs that start with "discord.gg" and then end with a series of randomly-selected upper-case letters, lower-case letters, and numbers. Many communities and projects use Discord servers to communicate with users, and sometimes, a Discord invite link on an official GitHub repository or website may have been misconfigured to expire, or it may simply be retired once the project has completed development.

While it may seem innocuous at first, malicious actors are now exploiting this very behavior in order to spread malware over Discord, and in a way that looks rather convincing, too.

 Related
5 things you may already be doing that compromise your Windows security

There's no absolute way to protect your PC from security threats. However, there are some habits you should definitely get rid of ASAP.

2

People are exploiting expired Discord invites to spread malware

So, when we talk about randomly-generated Discord invite links, there are other types of invite links a user can generate. When a server is "boosted" by enough members ("Boosts" can be contributed via Discord Nitro subscribers or purchased separately to support a server), new features will be unlocked for that server. One feature, once a server reaches a Level 3 boost, enables the server's moderators to generate a vanity server invite URL, meaning that a custom suffix can be affixed to the "discord.gg" URL as a way of personalizing the invitation so that it better fits the server or is memorable to share with others. These only support lower-case letters and numbers, though, and if you try to add an upper-case letter to a vanity URL, Discord automatically bumps it down to a lower-case one.

The attack begins with a hacker who owns a Level 3 Boost server. They find a link to a reputable Discord server and note down the randomly-generated code at the end of the URL if all of the characters in the invite link are lowercase. Then, this invitation link can be monitored until it "dies off." A vanity link cannot be identical to a currently active invite link, hence the waiting period, but if a community server uses a vanity URL and then loses its level of boost, then someone could also snipe that vanity URL when it's available. So, either an expired temporary invite or a vanity URL that's been temporarily released can be copied. Now, an invite to a benign server points instead to a malicious one.

Once the unsuspecting victim arrives on the malicious server, they are greeted by a bot called "Safeguard." The bot will direct the target to a website, claiming it's to verify the user and allow them access to the server. When the user clicks the link, it quickly grabs the victim's username, avatar, and banner, then constructs a fake Discord login page with this information to make it look like the real deal. Many large community servers use these bots to protect against automated attacks and spammers, so this wouldn't come across as explicitly out of the ordinary.

However, things very quickly deviate from the standard verification process that most large servers have their users endure. The website that loads will display a button that reads "Verify." Clicking this will stealthily plant a PowerShell command onto the target's clipboard. The fake verification process then asks the victim to open the Windows Run dialogue, paste the "verification string" (which is actually a malicious PowerShell command to retrieve an installation file from an online source and execute it), and hit Enter. The installation file then downloads and installs two malicious apps after a few minutes: Skuld Stealer (which steals crypto wallet details) and AsyncRAT (a Remote Access Trojan, which allows for remote control of the target PC).

Fortunately, dodging this attack is simple. If a website ever asks you to open Windows Run to paste in a code, it's almost certainly trying to trick you into installing malware. As long as you leave the page and refuse to run the code, the attack can't initiate, and your PC will be safe. However, this is a sophisticated attack that, for the most part, appears to blend seamlessly into what you would expect to be a legitimate Discord server. Discord has now taken this particular bot offline, according to Check Point Research, but it's likely that similar attacks will be attempted in the future. And if this has inspired you to check out a different app, take a peek at the best chat alternatives that are better than Discord.

item.title: 6 niche Obsidian plugins I really like using
item.itunes:title: 6 niche Obsidian plugins I really like using
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Thanks to its unparalleled flexibility in note-taking and knowledge management, Obsidian has become an indispensable tool for many. While its core features are robust, the true power of Obsidian often lies in its vibrant community and the incredible plugins they develop. You have likely heard of popular ones like Calendar, Kanban, Excalidraw, and more, but what about the less-talked-about gems?

Let’s dive into six niche Obsidian plugins that have enhanced my workflow. They prove that sometimes, the most impactful tools are the ones you least expect.

 Related
I replaced all productivity tools with Obsidian for a month and here is how it went

My 30-day Obsidian experiment

3

6 Charts

Add charts and graphs to your pages

This plugin is a game-changer for anyone who loves data and wants to visualize it directly within their notes. Before I found Charts, I was constantly using external tools and then pasting images back into Obsidian. It was clunky and broke my flow. This plugin changed all that.

What I love about Charts is its sheer simplicity and power. It allows me to define my data directly in a code block within an Obsidian note, and then, using a simple syntax, it renders beautiful, interactive charts. Whether it’s a bar chart tracking my daily water intake, a line graph showing my productivity trends, or a pie chart breaking down my project time, this plugin handles it all.

The fact that the data lives right alongside my notes is powerful. If I update a number in my data, the chart automatically refreshes.

5 PDF++

Unlock a PDF editor in Obsidian

Before I discovered PDF++, working with PDFs in Obsidian was a constant struggle. I had to open them in an external reader, highlight them, and then copy and paste notes back into Obsidian. With PDF++, it truly transformed how I handle academic papers, reports, and any other PDF document.

PDF++ basically acts as a complement to Obsidian’s native PDF viewer rather than replacing it. I can choose different colors for different types of information, which is a lifesaver for organizing my thoughts when reading complex documents.

4 Style Settings

Give your Obsidian vault a visual makeover

If you are someone who loves to customize the look and feel of your Obsidian vault, then Style Settings is an absolute non-negotiable. What I have noticed is that almost every well-developed third-party plugin now relies heavily on Style Settings. Theme developers integrate their customizable options directly into this plugin.

For instance, I’m currently using the Encore theme, and I have adjusted the heading sizes, changed the link colors, refined the note width, and more using the Style Settings plugin. And it’s not just themes, many other plugins like the PDF++ also expose their styling options through Style Settings. Check out my dedicated guide on creating an aesthetically pleasing Obsidian vault using Style Settings.

 Related
Here’s how I created a beautiful and functional Obsidian vault

Give your Obsidian vault a visual makeover

3 Beautitab

Add a home page to Obsidian

I use Beautitab to create a truly customizable new tab experience within Obsidian. Instead of just a blank page, I’m greeted with a beautiful background image. I can choose from a curated collection or even use my own. It’s a small detail, but it instantly makes opening a new panel feel more inviting. It’s like having a personalized dashboard every time I create a fresh workspace.

But it’s not just about pretty pictures. I have configured my Beautitab to display an inspirational quote and quick links to frequently accessed notes with the current time. Overall, Beautitab is one of those plugins that enhances the overall feel of using Obsidian. It’s a subtle but significant improvement to my daily interaction.

2 Callout Manager

Draw attention to specific information

The first thing I loved about Callout Manager plugin was its ability to offer a list of available callouts. I can quickly see all the standard Obsidian callouts, as well as any special ones introduced by themes. It’s like having a visual dictionary for all the callouts I can possibly use.

I can easily change the colors and icons of existing callouts. I don’t have to guess hex codes or hunt for icon names. Suppose I want my callout to be a specific shade of blue with a different icon, I can do it visually within the plugin’s settings.

And then there is the convenience of creating custom callouts. This is where my creativity really shines. If I need a specific type of information highlighted – say a [question-for-later] callout with a unique color and a question mark icon, I can build it from scratch right inside Callout Manager.

1 Chronology

A handy calendar view for your notes

Whether I’m tracking the development of a project, outlining historical events for a research paper, or even simply logging personal milestones, understanding the sequence of events is important. This is where the Chronology plugin has become invaluable for me.

It basically unlocks a calendar view where I can scroll to check my created notes chronologically. I have pinned it in the top right corner of my Obsidian vault.

My Obsidian game changers

These seven niche Obsidian plugins, though perhaps not as widely known, have become crucial in my daily workflow. Don’t limit yourself to these plugins, though. Obsidian has a vast plugin ecosystem. You shouldn’t be afraid to dig deeper into the plugin library. You might just uncover the exact functionality you didn’t even know you were searching for.

Aside from these plugins, I also use Obsidian with NotebookLM to get useful insights from my vaults.

Obsidian
Download Obsidian
